## [00:00 - 03:05]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about your market's biggest needs. Most great webinars provide a solution to one or more core problems that their avatar faces. So how do you figure those needs out? Well, personally, I like to start with brainstorming. Based on the knowledge you have of your audience and avatar, you can probably brainstorm a list in an hour or two that will contain most of their needs. This is that list for this particular program, where I just sat down, I was on a plane, and I jotted down a group of needs that I felt that people who were trying to succeed with webinars were having. And that was the creation of this list. Now, you should recognize that you should be able to sit down and quickly brainstorm a list of major needs that your audience has. And that list should be complete enough that you probably get 80% of their needs just out of your head. If you're not able to do that, you may not know your audience well enough to be able to be ready to be doing a webinar for them. So I just invite you to consider that. If I can't identify most of their needs off the top of my head, perhaps I'm not yet ready and don't yet have the expertise to be able to teach them something that they will really value. But once we get that done, then we back it up with information. We go and do Google research. Blog posts on the subject are especially useful. So I already showed you in a previous video, a search on female on the needs of female entrepreneurs, that kind of thing where you're going through blog posts and other articles that list those needs can be especially useful and can oftentimes add things to your list that you might not have had on your list. Another great place to look at look is podcast episodes. If there is a podcast or two that specifically addresses the avatar and the need that you are working with, those can be great tools for you to come to a greater nuanced understanding of what the problems are and how they're trying to deal with them. Another great place is other people's sales pages. So sales pages oftentimes are problem solution oriented. And so many sales people pages will start with defining, here's this set of problems, and my product solves those problems for you. Going through those sales pages can oftentimes help to flesh out your list of problems that your target audience is dealing with. Then you also have other online information sources.

## [03:06 - 06:10]

One of my favorite is a little site called Quora.com. Quora is a site where people ask questions and other people answer them. Now it's got a lot of answers that aren't very valuable, but there are some other answers that are amazingly valuable. And so going to that site and searching for questions, just searching on your key terms will oftentimes give you several key questions that will help you to understand your audience better. Another online information source is sites serving your vertical market. So the female entrepreneur market, there are a number of sites that specifically serve that that audience. And just going through and looking at what they've written about, what they're talking about, the problems that they're solving with products and everything else like that can help you to flesh out this list. Facebook groups and LinkedIn groups are also very, very powerful places to go join those groups, hang out in them, watch what people are complaining about, watch what people are asking for help in and learn what they are showing you there. Twitter, searching especially for relevant hashtags. So go find in Twitter, some of the people that are serving this market and that are participating this up market, read their post, look at hashtags to discover things that that are there. And then you've got all kinds of other things. You got Pinterest, you got Instagram, you got lots of other places where people are living their lives and talking about their lives and all of those can give you insights. Next, I suggest you pick up the phone. Who do you know that fits your avatar, pick up the phone and call them. Hey, I'm working on a new product and I just want to ask you a few questions. Or let's say you've got a bunch of things already figured out, I just want to check a few things with you. So then you say, here's what I've been thinking, what do you think? And then absolutely listen to them, don't argue with them. The purpose of this call is to learn from them, not to try to rationalize the choices that you've made. Now, hopefully you also have an email list made up of people who are in your target avatar, sending out a survey to them. I recommend that you send out fill in the blank questions. So not ones where people check a box, but fill in the blank questions. So what's your biggest challenge? Or what's your biggest frustration? Or what's your biggest issue with? And so you focus them on the subject area that you're talking about. So if you ask, what's your biggest challenge in life, they may say, I got arthritis. Well, unless you're talking about arthritis, that may not help you. But if you ask, what's your biggest challenge with being a female entrepreneur with young kids living at home? Alright, now you're going to get some answers that

## [06:10 - 08:57]

are valuable. And then the next question is, what else do you find yourself struggling with? So you just ask them what's your biggest challenge? Now ask them, what else do you find yourself struggling with? Now, I realize that the statisticians, I have a minor in statistics, I understand what I'm telling you. I'm telling you, Hey, basically, you're going to get a bunch of free formed text responses where people are just going to write back to you. That's not scalable. If you got 2000 of those, you'd be struggling to get the best information out of them. But here's what'll happen if you get 2000 answers and you start going through them very quickly, you're going to realize, Oh, that's a problem. And that one keeps coming up. Oh, and we shouldn't forget that one. And you'll be able to see it very, very quickly. So that's why I recommend open ended, fill in the blank questions, where people are where you're just asking them to share what they're struggling with. So your mission is to identify one to three important challenges that you can actually solve for them in your webinar. Now realize that that's a fairly tight group of things because you probably cannot create world peace in a 90 minute webinar with 100 people on it. So these need to be challenges that lots of people have that are hard to solve that you can help them solve and that you can teach them that solution a fairly fairly short period of time like five to 10 minutes each. And those problems to choose, you want to choose ones that prepare them to buy your product later on in the webinar. So one problem with many webinars is they don't try to solve a specific problem. The problem that they teach isn't something that people really want solved. It's not compelling. Or they give tips, not solutions. Or they don't drill into how that problem is affecting the viewer's life before they solve it. So if you just come on and say, Hey, here's this solution, your problem, and you don't talk about how that problem is affecting them, you lose a huge psychological element that you're going to want to build on later on in your webinar, or the solution that they provide isn't believable. So those are all problems. Please don't make those mistakes. But let's choose a series of one to three problems that are compelling. Lots of people have that you can actually solve and that you can actually solve in the short period of a webinar. And then you've got yourself a set of things you can talk about in your webinar, they'll be incredibly powerful.
